Adds "a2a-samples" as a dependency in `hosts/cli/pyproject.toml` and `hosts/multiagent/pyproject.toml`. Also, fixes `uv.lock` being out of sync (was missing some dev-dependencies).

Discovered this issue after running `uv sync` in a freshly cloned copy.

## Details

After the "splitting subproject-specific and common dependencies" change was applied (PR #182), I wanted to verify that environments worked as expected. So, I cloned the `main` branch into a new location (with no preexisting `.venv`'s) and after running `uv sync` noticed that `uv.lock` was out of sync and the "a2a-samples" dependency was missing in `hosts/cli` and `hosts/multiagent`. While the `multiagent` still worked from `demo/ui` because `demo/ui` directly requires "a2a-samples," the CLI client did not.

With this fix, the CLI client works as expected.

### uv version:

* uv 0.6.14

---

Refs: basically, this is a follow-up to PR #182

author: egor-baranov
title: Fix error: invoking agent: 'generator' object is not subscriptable
raw_text:
I had this type of exception in my local sources while running this ADK example

```json
INFO:common.server.task_manager:Upserting task b7ebdb35-a1ad-4b57-920e-4112b69b2a50 ERROR:task_manager:Error invoking agent: 'generator' object is not subscriptable ERROR:common.server.server:Unhandled exception: Error invoking agent: 'generator' object is not subscriptable
INFO:     ::1:60297 - "POST / HTTP/1.1" 400 Bad Request
```

And actually the run method signature is

```python
def run(
      self,
      *,
      user_id: str,
      session_id: str,
      new_message: types.Content,
      run_config: RunConfig = RunConfig(),
  ) -> Generator[Event, None, None]:
```

So it returns the Generator object which is not subscriptable as it is trying to be in this code example

Fix me if there is a easier solution but this approach helped me to run E2E implementation with my custom UI in TypeScript and agent made with Python

author: egor-baranov
title: Part (TextPart | FilePart | DataPart) type property should't be optional
raw_text:
When we're doing requests between TypeScript client and Python server there are some problem related to types:

```typescript
Part = TextPart | FilePart | DataPart 
```
in a client side contains an optional property type that doesn't go when doing request to a backend.

But on a Python server side there are a strict type validation

```python
Part = Annotated[Union[TextPart, FilePart, DataPart], Field(discriminator="type")]
```

That throws an exception when type is not provided 

```json
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "error": {
    "code": -32600,
    "message": "Request payload validation error",
    "data": [
      {
        "type": "union_tag_not_found",
        "loc": [
          "tasks/send",
          "params",
          "message",
          "parts",
          0
        ],
        "msg": "Unable to extract tag using discriminator 'type'",
        "input": {
          "text": "hi"
        },
        "ctx": {
          "discriminator": "'type'"
        },
        "url": "https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.10/v/union_tag_not_found"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```
So this is why we need to make it required. Also in a python side this field is not optional 

```python
class TextPart(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["text"] = "text"
    text: str
    metadata: dict[str, Any] | None = None
```

author: egor-baranov
title: Implement /agent-card endpoint presented in client.ts
raw_text:
In file /samples/js/src/client/client.ts there are an agentCard method that triggers a GET request trying to retrieve this data. But from the server-side it's defined only in /.well-known/agent.json. So we need to additionally implement the same method through endpoint compatible with specification

https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/71b5a485a9cf844a531e8a8c821507d9358dd4b9/samples/js/src/client/client.ts#L362

author: darkhaniop
title: Split common and agent-specific dependencies
raw_text:
This PR addresses a problem described in issue #181, to help:
* reduce bloating dependency trees of new agent projects (both in a2a-samples and external);
* establish better patterns for adding new agent examples to this repository;
* help reduce merge conflicts by lessening the need to touch `samples/python/pyproject.toml` due to updates in sample agents/clients.

## Summary

Moves subproject-specific, e.g. `agents/langgraph`, dependencies into the respective directories:
* Adds `agents/googl_adk/pyproject.toml`.
* Adds `agents/langgraph/pyproject.toml`.
* Adds `hosts/cli/pyproject.toml`.
* Adds `hosts/multiagent/pyproject.toml` and a short readme.
* Updates dependencies in the upper-level `samples/python/pyproject.toml`.
* Updates sample agent run instructions in `samples/python/README.md`.

This PR adds a new Daytona Sandbox Orchestration Agent implementation to the A2A protocol repository.

- Iintroduces a Python-based **Daytona** agent under `samples/python/agents/daytona/`
  - the agent exposes sandbox orchestration capabilities via the A2A protocol, including:
    - creating sandboxes with configurable resources (CPU, memory, disk, language, environment variables, auto-stop interval)
    - starting, stopping, and destroying sandboxes
    - listing all sandboxes
    - executing shell commands and running code within a sandbox
  - implements a `DaytonaSandboxAgent` class that wraps Daytona SDK operations and exposes them as A2A agent skills

- **sample usage and demo:**  
  - provided a `README.md` with setup instructions and a multi-agent demo scenario where the Daytona agent works with a Coder agent to generate and safely execute code in an isolated environment

#### Why Add a Daytona Agent

- enables secure, isolated execution of AI-generated code and commands as part of multi-agent workflows, addressing a key requirement for safe and scalable agent interoperability
- demonstrates how specialized infrastructure agents can be integrated into the A2A ecosystem to extend agent capabilities without compromising security

---
**Files added:**
- `samples/python/agents/daytona/README.md`
- `samples/python/agents/daytona/__main__.py`
- `samples/python/agents/daytona/agent.py`
- `samples/python/agents/daytona/task_manager.py`
- `samples/python/agents/daytona/__init__.py`

author: djsamseng
title: Export google_a2a as a python module instead of common and agents
raw_text:
Sets up the file structure correctly to allow for the modules to be imported with a proper namespace.

Previously developers would have to
```python
import common
```

Now developers can
```python
import google_a2a.common
```

This will allow us to further enhance this module to become production ready in the future. And in the meantime it allows those of us experimenting with A2A to avoid namespace clashes.

See the samples/python/README.md for an example setup.

Addresses the issues raised [in this comment](https://github.com/google/A2A/issues/148#issuecomment-2802282457)

Test Plan:
- Note I don't have a GOOGLE_API_KEY so I couldn't fully run, but it exit cleanly thus verifying the only changes (the top level imports)
```bash
uv run src/google_a2a/agents/langgraph
uv run src/google_a2a/hosts/cli
```
- Additionally tested importing google_a2a from another project via `uv add /path/to/A2A/samples/python` and was able to `import google_a2a.common.server` and create and run an `A2AServer`. This verifies the server does in fact run.

author: HaohanTsao
title: Add Google API Key Configuration in demo app UI
raw_text: ## Problem Currently, the A2A demo application requires the `GOOGLE_API_KEY` environment variable to be set before startup. If not set, the application exits immediately. Users have no way to set or update their API key through the UI. ## Solution This PR adds UI components to allow users to enter and update their Google API Key directly in the application. It includes: 1. API Key input dialog for initial setup 2. Settings page integration for subsequent updates 3. Success notification feedback after updates 4. Server-side handling to apply API key changes without requiring application restart ## Changed Files ### Core State Management - `demo/ui/state/state.py`: Added API key related state properties to AppState class ### UI Components - `demo/ui/components/api_key_dialog.py`: Created new dialog component for API key input - `demo/ui/pages/settings.py`: Added API key configuration section in settings page ### Server and API Integration - `demo/ui/main.py`: Modified startup logic to show API key dialog instead of exiting - `demo/ui/service/server/server.py`: Added API endpoint for updating API key - `demo/ui/service/server/adk_host_manager.py`: Modified to accept API key parameter and support updates - `demo/ui/state/host_agent_service.py`: Added UpdateApiKey function to communicate with server ## Screenshots ### Initial API Key Dialog *Dialog shown when application starts without API key set* ### API Key Settings Section *API Key configuration in Settings page* ### Success Notification *Feedback shown after successful API key update* ## Testing 1. Start the application without GOOGLE_API_KEY set in environment 2. Verify the API key dialog appears 3. Enter a valid Google API key and click Save 4. Verify the application works normally with entered API key 5. Go to Settings page and update the API key 6. Verify success notification appears and the new API key is used 7. Verify the application works normally after updating API key Resolves #50

author: vladislavdonchev
title: FEATURE: Compliance and Distributed Knowledge Extension to A2A Protocol
raw_text:
# FEATURE: Adding Regulatory Compliance and Collaborative Knowledge Graph Support to the A2A Protocol ![Banner Image](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Independent-AI-Labs/A2A/main/images/A2A_kg_extension_banner.png) ## **Context & Motivation:** This PR introduces a significant extension to the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, enabling agents to collaboratively build, query, and react to shared structured knowledge using Knowledge Graphs (KGs). Advanced agentic collaboration requires sharing and accessing dynamic operational knowledge in a safe and verifiable way. This extension defines standardized methods for KG query, update, and subscription, providing enhanced protocol-level reasoning, alignment, and multi-agent understanding functionality. It prominently features support for GraphQL as the primary query language. A secondary objective of the extension is enabling **A2A** protocol compliance with major security and AI frameworks such as the **EU AI Act**, **NIST AI Risk Management Framework** (**RMF**), **ISO/IEC** standards (including **42001** for **AI Management Systems** and **23894** for **AI Risk Management**), and ethical guidelines like the **Australian AI Ethics Framework**, by providing standardized mechanisms for auditability, provenance tracking, capability declaration, and verifiable knowledge updates. A complete analysis of the system's compliance parameters can be found in the guide here: - [A2A COMPLIANCE GUIDE (WIP)](https://github.com/Independent-AI-Labs/A2A/blob/main/A2A_COMPLIANCE_GUIDE.md) --- ## Client-Server Conceptual Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant ClientAgent as Agent A (Client) participant ServerAgent as Agent B (Server w/ KG) ClientAgent->>+ServerAgent: knowledge/query (GraphQL Query) ServerAgent->>-ClientAgent: knowledge/query Response (GraphQL Result) ClientAgent->>+ServerAgent: knowledge/update (KG Patches, Justification) ServerAgent-->>ServerAgent: Verify Patches (Alignment Check) ServerAgent->>-ClientAgent: knowledge/update Response (Success/Fail, Verification Status) ClientAgent->>+ServerAgent: knowledge/subscribe (GraphQL Subscription) ServerAgent-->>ServerAgent: Establish Subscription Note over ServerAgent: KG Change Occurs ServerAgent-->>ClientAgent: SSE Stream: KnowledgeSubscriptionEvent (Change Data) ServerAgent-->>ClientAgent: SSE Stream: ... (More Events) ... ``` ## Peer-to-Peer Conceptual Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AgentA as 💡 Agent A participant AgentB as 🧩 Agent B participant AgentC as 🌍 Agent C Note over AgentA, AgentC: Agents collaborating on 'Task T' %% --- Agent A needs context from Agent B --- AgentA ->>+ AgentB: knowledge/query
query: "{ task(id: 'T') { context } }" activate AgentB Note over AgentB: Queries internal KG for Task T context AgentB ->>- AgentA: Response
result: { "data": { "task": { "context": "Initial details..." } } } deactivate AgentB %% --- Agent C discovers new relevant info and shares it with peers --- Note over AgentC: Discovers Fact F relevant to Task T AgentC ->>+ AgentA: knowledge/update
mutations: [ {op: "add", statement: } ],
sourceAgentId: "AgentC", taskId: "T" activate AgentA AgentA -->> AgentA: Verify & Integrate Fact F AgentA ->>- AgentC: Response
result: { success: true, verificationStatus: "Verified" } deactivate AgentA AgentC ->>+ AgentB: knowledge/update
mutations: [ {op: "add", statement: } ],
sourceAgentId: "AgentC", taskId: "T" activate AgentB AgentB -->> AgentB: Verify & Integrate Fact F AgentB ->>- AgentC: Response
result: { success: true, verificationStatus: "Verified" } deactivate AgentB %% --- Agent B was subscribing to changes related to Task T from Agent A --- Note over AgentA, AgentB: Agent B previously subscribed to Agent A
for updates related to Task T activate AgentA Note over AgentA: Integrating Fact F triggers subscription event AgentA -->> AgentB: SSE Stream: KnowledgeSubscriptionEvent
result: { op: "add", statement: } deactivate AgentA %% --- Agent B reacts to the update and queries Agent C for details --- activate AgentB Note over AgentB: Processes Fact F via subscription, needs clarification AgentB ->>+ AgentC: knowledge/query
query: "{ factDetails(id: 'Fact F ID') { sourceCertainty } }" activate AgentC Note over AgentC: Provides details based on its observation AgentC ->>- AgentB: Response
result: { "data": { "factDetails": { "sourceCertainty": 0.9 } } } deactivate AgentC deactivate AgentB Note over AgentA, AgentC: Collaboration on Task T continues... ``` --- ## **Description of Changes:** 1. **`common/types.py`:** * Added new Pydantic models: `KGSubject`, `KGPredicate`, `KGObject`, `KGStatement`, `PatchOperationType`, `KnowledgeGraphPatch`. * Added parameter models: `KnowledgeQueryParams`, `KnowledgeUpdateParams`, `KnowledgeSubscribeParams`. * Added request models: `KnowledgeQueryRequest`, `KnowledgeUpdateRequest`, `KnowledgeSubscribeRequest`. * Added response/result models: `KnowledgeQueryResponse`, `KnowledgeQueryResponseResult`, `KnowledgeUpdateResponse`, `KnowledgeUpdateResponseResult`. * Added streaming event models: `KnowledgeGraphChangeEvent`, `KnowledgeSubscriptionEvent`. * Added new error types: `KnowledgeQueryError`, `KnowledgeUpdateError`, `KnowledgeSubscriptionError`, `AlignmentViolationError`. * Updated `A2ARequest` Union type to include new knowledge requests. * Added `knowledgeGraph` and `knowledgeGraphQueryLanguages` to `AgentCapabilities`. 2. **`a2a.json`:** * Updated JSON schema to include definitions for all new types, requests, responses, errors, and capabilities listed above. * Ensured schema reflects GraphQL support (`queryLanguage` enum, `variables`, flexible `data` structure in response). 3. **`client.py` (`A2AClient`):** * Added new async methods: `knowledge_query`, `knowledge_update`, `knowledge_subscribe`. * `knowledge_subscribe` returns an `AsyncIterable[KnowledgeSubscriptionEvent]` using SSE. * Added capability checks before calling new methods. 4. **`server.py` (`A2AServer`):** * Updated `_process_request` to route new `knowledge/*` methods based on `AgentCapabilities`. * Added specific error handling for `MethodNotFoundError` if capabilities are missing. * Updated `A2ARequest` validation usage. * SSE stream generation logic in `_create_response` now handles `KnowledgeSubscriptionEvent` responses. 5. **`task_manager.py` (`TaskManager`, `InMemoryTaskManager`):** * Added abstract method signatures for `on_knowledge_query`, `on_knowledge_update`, `on_knowledge_subscribe` to the `TaskManager` ABC. * Provided placeholder implementations in `InMemoryTaskManager` that return `UnsupportedOperationError`. *(Note: Real implementation requires KG/GraphQL backend)*. * Added placeholder for `knowledge_sse_subscribers` dictionary. **Key Features Implemented:** * Protocol definition for KG Query, Update, and Subscription methods. * Explicit support for GraphQL (`queryLanguage`, `variables`). * Standardized data models for KG statements and patches. * Incorporation of specialized safety and compliance concepts: `certainty`, `provenance`, `justification`, `verificationStatus`, `metadata` fields. * Capability flags (`knowledgeGraph`, `knowledgeGraphQueryLanguages`) in `AgentCard`. * Client methods for utilizing the new features. * Server routing and basic capability checks for new methods. * Updated JSON Schema specification. **Implementation Notes:** * The server-side logic in `InMemoryTaskManager` for handling the actual KG operations and GraphQL execution is **not implemented** in this PR (placeholders returning `UnsupportedOperationError`). A concrete agent implementation will need to provide this using appropriate backend libraries (e.g., RDFLib + Ariadne). * Subscription management (tracking subscribers, matching changes to queries) is also part of the backend implementation left for concrete agents. **Testing:** * Added/updated unit tests for new types in `common/types.py`. * Added basic unit tests for new `A2AClient` methods (checking request formation). * Added basic tests for `A2AServer` routing of new methods (checking capability errors and routing to manager stubs). * *TODO:* Integration tests require a mock or real agent implementation with KG/GraphQL capabilities. **Reviewer Guidance:** * Please review the API design of the new methods (`knowledge/*`). * Check the correctness and completeness of the new Pydantic types and the JSON schema (`a2a.json`). * Verify the client method implementations and server routing logic. --- # 🚀 Enhancing A2A: Knowledge Graph Collaboration ## The Need for Structured Knowledge While the base A2A protocol excels at task-based communication, complex AI collaboration requires a deeper, shared understanding of the world – entities, concepts, relationships, and processes. Knowledge Graphs offer a flexible and powerful way to represent this structured information. This extension provides the standard mechanisms for agents to leverage KGs within the A2A framework. ## Core Features This extension introduces a new `knowledge/` namespace with methods that allow agents to: 1. ❓ **Query Knowledge (`knowledge/query`)** * Ask questions of another agent's KG using standardized query languages. * **GraphQL First:** Explicit support for **GraphQL** queries and variables. * *Example:* "Find all tasks assigned to 'agent-x' that are currently 'working'." 2. ✍️ **Update Knowledge (`knowledge/update`)** * Propose additions or removals of specific facts (statements/triples) to another agent's KG. * Includes fields for `justification` and `sourceAgentId` for provenance and auditability. * Receiving agent verifies updates against its internal rules and alignment constraints. * *Example:* "Add the fact that 'task-123' has produced 'artifact-abc'." 3. 🔔 **Subscribe to Knowledge Changes (`knowledge/subscribe`)** * Receive real-time notifications (via Server-Sent Events) when specific parts of an agent's KG change. * Uses **GraphQL Subscriptions** to define the data of interest. * *Example:* "Notify me whenever the status of any task associated with 'project-alpha' changes." --- ## Reasoning, Safety & Compliance * **Cognitive Mapping & Symbolic Reasoning:** Can directly represent the symbolic relationships modeled in knowledge bases, cognitive maps, and structured / discrete spaces. * **Multi-Agent Reasoning:** Provides the foundation for shared understanding needed for complex collaborative reasoning. * **Secure Collaboration:** `metadata` fields allow secure context passing; update verification enforces rules. * **Alignment & Auditability:** `provenance`, `certainty`, `justification`, and `verificationStatus` fields support verifiable and aligned AI behavior. ## Benefits * **Richer Agent Interaction:** Moves beyond simple tasking to deep knowledge sharing. * **Improved Contextual Awareness:** Agents can query for relevant background information. * **Enhanced Collaboration:** Enables joint knowledge base construction and maintenance. * **Standardization:** Provides a common protocol layer for diverse KG backends and GraphQL engines. * **Foundation for Advanced Machine Intelligence:** Supports capabilities like shared understanding, complex reasoning, and verifiable alignment. --- ## Next Steps - [x] Updating the TS/JS (web) sample server and client. - [ ] Creating a sample Python agent (TBD). - [ ] Creating a sample web agent (TBD). - [ ] Updating the demo UI with a knowledge-sharing workflow (TBD). - Implementing agents will require integrating KG storage (e.g., RDFLib, GraphDB, Neo4j) and a GraphQL engine (e.g., Ariadne, Graphene) into the A2A server implementation. ## Docs - [KNOWLEDGE GRAPH EXTENSION README](https://github.com/Independent-AI-Labs/A2A/blob/main/KNOWLEDGE_GRAPH_EXTENSION.md) - [COMPLIANCE GUIDE (WIP)](https://github.com/Independent-AI-Labs/A2A/blob/main/A2A_COMPLIANCE_GUIDE.md) ---

author: LingJingMaster
title: Fix Crewai_CLI default connection
raw_text:
## Issue Description
Currently, there is a port inconsistency between the CLI client and the CrewAI agent service. The CrewAI agent was previously using port 10001, while the CLI client in samples/python/hosts/cli is configured to use port 10000. This inconsistency causes connection failures when trying to use the CLI client with the CrewAI agent.

## Solution
~~Standardize the port usage across both components to use port 10000 consistently. This change ensures that the CLI client can connect to the CrewAI agent service without manual port configuration.~~

I've updated the documentation in `samples/python/agents/crewai/README.md` to explicitly specify the correct agent URL when running the CLI client


## Changes Made
~~- Modified the CrewAI agent to use port 10000 by default (`as seen in samples/python/agents/crewai/__main__.py`)~~
~~- Verified that the CLI client is already configured to use port 10000~~
```bash
   # Connect to the agent (specify the agent URL with correct port)
   uv run hosts/cli --agent http://localhost:10001
   # If you changed the port when starting the agent, use that port instead
   # uv run hosts/cli --agent http://localhost:YOUR_PORT
```

## Testing
Verified that the CLI client can successfully connect to the CrewAI agent service after this change.

author: LingJingMaster
title: Fix Crewai_CLI default connection port to use 10001 consistently
raw_text:
## Issue Description
Currently, there is a port inconsistency between the CLI client and the CrewAI agent service. The CrewAI agent was previously using port 10000, while the CLI client in samples/python/hosts/cli is configured to use port 10001. This inconsistency causes connection failures when trying to use the CLI client with the CrewAI agent.

## Solution
Standardize the port usage across both components to use port 10001 consistently. This change ensures that the CLI client can connect to the CrewAI agent service without manual port configuration.

## Changes Made
- Modified the CrewAI agent to use port 10001 by default (`as seen in samples/python/agents/crewai/__main__.py`)
- Verified that the CLI client is already configured to use port 10001

## Testing
Verified that the CLI client can successfully connect to the CrewAI agent service after this change.

author: iamrajiv
title: feat(samples): Add go language sample for `A2A` protocol
raw_text:
## Description

Fixes: google-a2a/a2a-samples#31

Provides a starting point for developers wanting to implement A2A agents or clients using Go.

- Adds a new `samples/go` directory containing the sample code.
- Follows a modular structure:
  - `schema`: Defines Go structs matching the A2A JSON-RPC specification.
  - `store`: Provides a `TaskStore` interface and an `InMemoryTaskStore` implementation.
  - `agent`: Contains the core `A2AHandler` implementing a basic "echo" agent for `tasks/send` and `tasks/get` methods over HTTP.
  - `main`: Sets up the HTTP server, initializes dependencies (logger, store, handler).
- Uses the standard Go `log/slog` package for structured JSON logging.
- Includes `go.mod` for dependency management.

author: Guidosalimbeni
title: pydanticai example
raw_text:
feat: Add Pydantic AI Summarizer Agent with A2A Integration

This pull request introduces the `SummarizerAgent`, a new agent built using Pydantic AI, showcasing how to integrate this library with the A2A framework. The `SummarizerAgent` is designed to take a text string as input and return a concise summary of that text.

**Motivation:**

This example provides a clear illustration of how Pydantic AI can be leveraged to create agents and how such agents can be easily integrated into the A2A ecosystem.

**Changes:**

-   **`samples/python/agents/pydantic_agent/agent.py`:**
    -   Defines the `SummarizerAgent` class, inheriting from Pydantic's `BaseModel`.
    -   Utilizes Pydantic AI's `@AICallable` decorator to define the `summarize` method, which handles the summarization logic.
    -   Configures the agent to use the OpenAI API key fetched from the `OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable.
    -   The summarize function has a string as input, and a string as output.
-   **`samples/python/agents/pydantic_agent/__main__.py`:**
    -   Creates an instance of the `SummarizerAgent`.
    -   Wraps the agent instance in an `A2AAgent` to make it compatible with the A2A protocol.
    -   Starts an `A2AServer` to host the agent and handle incoming A2A requests.
-   **`samples/python/agents/pydantic_agent/README.md`:**
    -   Provides instructions on how to run the agent locally.
    -   Explains how to connect to the agent.
    -   Describes the input and output of the agent.
-   **`samples/python/agents/pydantic_agent/__init__.py`**:
    - Empty file, to make the module a python module.

**How to Run and Test:**

1.  Ensure the `OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable is set.
2.  Navigate to the `samples/python/agents/pydantic_agent/` directory.
3.  Execute `python __main__.py`.
4.  Find the agent card url printed on the console.
5. Add the agent to the demo UI or any other A2A client.
6. You will be able to interact with the agent, sending texts and getting summaries.

**Benefits:**

-   Provides a practical example of using Pydantic AI for agent development.
-   Demonstrates the integration of a Pydantic AI agent with the A2A framework.

author: binbandit
title: feat: add `rejected` task state to a2a protocol
raw_text:
This PR adds a new 'rejected' task state to the A2A protocol, addressing issue #104.

## Problem
Currently, the A2A protocol has states for tasks that are completed, canceled, or failed, but there's no way to explicitly indicate that a task was rejected. This makes it difficult to distinguish between tasks that failed due to errors and tasks that were explicitly rejected by the agent.

## Changes
1. Added the "rejected" state to the TaskState enum in the JSON schema (`specification/json/a2a.json`)
2. Added the "rejected" state to the TaskState enum in the Python implementation (`samples/python/common/types.py`)
3. Added the "rejected" state to the TaskState type in the TypeScript implementation (`samples/js/src/schema.ts`)

## Testing
The changes have been tested by:
1. Verifying that the Python implementation correctly defines and uses the "rejected" state
2. Verifying that the TypeScript implementation correctly defines the "rejected" state
3. Validating that the JSON schema remains valid after the changes

## Documentation
No documentation updates were needed as the task states are primarily defined in the schema files that have been updated.

author: binbandit
title: fix: Update UI components to respect dark mode theme
raw_text:
This PR fixes issue #108 where the dark mode in the web UI is not working correctly. Some UI components had hardcoded colors that didn't respect the theme settings.

## Problem
Several UI components had hardcoded background colors like `#fff`, `whitesmoke`, `lightgreen`, and `smokewhite` instead of using theme variables. This caused these components to display with light colors even when dark mode was enabled, creating a poor user experience.

## Changes
1. Updated `dialog.py` to use `me.theme_var("background")` instead of hardcoded `#fff` for dialog backgrounds
2. Updated `form_render.py` to use `me.theme_var("surface")` instead of hardcoded `whitesmoke` for form backgrounds
3. Updated `chat_bubble.py` to use theme variables for chat bubble backgrounds:
   - Changed `lightgreen` and `lightgrey` to `me.theme_var("primary-container")` and `me.theme_var("secondary-container")`
   - Changed `smokewhite` to `me.theme_var("secondary-container")`

## Testing
The changes have been tested by toggling between light and dark modes. All components now properly respect the theme settings, providing a consistent dark mode experience.

## Screenshots
Before:
![Dark mode issue](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dc502470-1845-4bb2-980a-b67adc7632a8)

After:

author: binbandit
title: fix: Prioritize message history over structured output in LangGraph agent
raw_text:
This PR fixes issue google-a2a/a2a-samples#117 where there's a mismatch between the AI response in the message history and the structured output in the LangGraph example.

## Problem
When using `create_react_agent` with a structured output format, LangGraph runs the model twice:
1. First with `bind_tools` to get a response that knows about the tools (stored in message history)
2. Then with `with_structured_output` to get a structured response (stored in structured_response)

These two responses can be inconsistent, as mentioned in issue google-a2a/a2a-samples#117. The first response (with tools) appears in the message history, while the second response (structured) appears in the `structured_response` field.

## Solution
The fix modifies the `get_agent_response` method to prioritize the message from the message history over the structured output when they conflict:

```python
# Get the last message from the message history
# This is the response from the first LLM call with bind_tools
messages = current_state.values.get('messages', [])
last_message_content = None
if messages and len(messages) > 0:
    last_message = messages[-1]
    if isinstance(last_message, AIMessage) and last_message.content:
        last_message_content = last_message.content

# Use the message from the message history if available, otherwise use the structured response
message_content = last_message_content or structured_response.message
```

## Testing
I created a simple test script to verify that the fix works as expected. The test confirms that:

1. The original implementation uses only the structured content, which can be inconsistent with the message history
2. The fixed implementation prioritizes the message content from the message history, which is more likely to be correct

This fix ensures that the response shown to the user is consistent with the tool usage and reasoning shown in the message history, which is important for a good user experience.

author: binbandit
title: fix: Convert generator to list before indexing in ADK agent
raw_text:
This PR fixes issue google-a2a/a2a-samples#38 where the ADK agent was attempting to index a generator object directly, which is not supported in Python.

## Problem
In the `invoke` method of the `ReimbursementAgent` class, the code was trying to access `events[-1]` where `events` is a generator returned by `self._runner.run()`. Generators don't support indexing, which would cause a TypeError.

## Solution
The fix converts the generator to a list before attempting to index it:
```python
events = list(self._runner.run(
    user_id=self._user_id, session_id=session.id, new_message=content
))

title: fix(format): format demo and sample code with ruff
raw_text:
The current codebase includes inconsistent formatting and many unused imports.

This PR applies [ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/), which is listed in the [dev dependencies](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/251cd37d5e6ccbc985bb8d0d78c03d665fa86699/demo/ui/pyproject.toml#L34) to:
- Format the Python code
- Sort and fix import statements
- Remove unused imports

Note: the code style expectations are not clearly defined in the contributing guide. It’s unclear whether the project intends to follow [Google Python Style](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html), use [yapf](https://github.com/google/yapf), or rely on ruff.
Since ruff is already present in the pyproject.toml, I’ve assumed it’s intended to be used for formatting.

If that’s not the case, feel free to close this PR or suggest the preferred formatting tool.

author: AlexsJones
title: feature: Golang sample implementation
raw_text:
Okay, I got very caffeinated and decided we need something in Golang, because this was the only way I could wrap my head around this. The PR introduces a Go implementation of the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, focusing on the core server and client components. It does not yet cover additional golang agents ( I'll do that in another PR if you accept this ).

CLA signed... 


## Changes

### Server Implementation
Implemented a JSON-RPC 2.0 server/client
Added support for core A2A methods:
tasks/send: Send new tasks
tasks/get: Retrieve task status
tasks/cancel: Cancel running tasks
Implemented thread-safe task storage
Error handling with A2A error codes etc
Created extensive test coverage for server functionality
### Client Implementation
 support for all core A2A methods
Created test suite for client (Thanks Copilot 👍)
### Models Package
Defined type-safe structures for:
Task management
Error handling
Implemented JSON serialization/deserialization
Added validation for all data structures
Added streaming support

### Documentation
Created README files for:
Server package
Client package
Models package
Added usage examples and API documentation
Included testing instructions

## Pending Work
Agent implementation is not included in this PR and will be addressed in a future update
The current implementation focuses on the core protocol and infrastructure
Agent-specific functionality (like skill execution, capability negotiation, etc.) will be implemented separately.

title: feat: run the code in daytona
raw_text:
This PR adds a prompt for running the code that the coder generated - the code is run in a secure and isolated Daytona Sandbox that spins up in a fraction of a second and runs the arbitrary Python/TypeScript code

How to use it:

1. First terminal window:
`export GEMINI_API_KEY=<your-gemini-api-key>`
`npm run agents:coder`

2. Second terminal window:
`export DAYTONA_API_KEY=<your-daytona-api-key`
`npm run a2a:cli:daytona`

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3ace8fa4-4d63-44b5-bffa-3ae95abc6a46)


This is a simple addition to the Coder sample as a proof of work implementation of an AI runtime. We are working on an A2A implementation with our sandbox orchestration agent that will be released soon. Interested in the feedback from the team.

author: djsamseng
raw_text:
Isn't this a duplicate of `canceled` or `failed`? Couldn't this be implemented already?

A. If it's a single task synchronous request, then reject the request and don't create the task
```python
return SendTaskResponse(
  id=request.id,
  error=JSONRPCError(
    code=-32000, # Server error
    message=f"Server error: {str(e)}"
  )
)
```

B. If it's a subscription asynchronous request, then cancel the task that was already started (or reject the initial request before starting the asynchronous request which is the same as A.)

```python
task_status = TaskStatus(
  state=TaskState.CANCELED,
  message=Message(
    role="agent",
    parts=[
      typing.cast(Part, {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "No ollama agent running to process the request"
      })
    ]
  )
)
task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
  id=task_id,
  status=task_status,
  final=True,
)
await self.enqueue_events_for_sse(
  task_id=task_id,
  task_update_event=task_update_event,
)
```

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
By looking at all of the request-response examples, I noticed that `organizationToken` is obtained when registering the organization (no mention of other kinds of tokens), and the same token is used to manage the organization (update, add, and remove agents) as well as to discover non-public agents.

Unless an additional mechanism to issue tokens with limited scope/time is added, I think this "organization registration" response should include another token with **read-only permissions**. This additional token can then be distributed to the internal clients (e.g., partners, non-dev employees) to allow them to discover private agents.

What do you think?

author: sandyskies
raw_text: The organizationToken is similar to a admin token and can be implemented based on the OAuth Bearer token. Of course, each organization can issue some tokens controlled by RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) to others for use, such as read-only tokens, etc.

author: darkhaniop
raw_text: @sandyskies, it makes sense, thanks for the explanation.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
How about if we rephrase this to better clarify the contextId and Tasks.

- A Task is always created by the Remote Agent and the status is always determined by the Remote Agent.
- Client can bundle the communication to an agent within a context by specifying a contextId. All tasks created by the remote agent will be associated to that contextId.
- This contextId is optional. If not specified for a request, a fresh contextId is created by the Remote Agent.
- Remote Agent correlates the request with the other contextual information for that id and decides if it is creating a Task(s), resuming a task or simply responds with a Message.

author: Mocuto
raw_text:
Please see the original issue https://github.com/google/A2A/issues/104. I will briefly resummarize the rationale:
1. It's in your own documentation that it is possible for a task to be rejected by the server
2. Rejecting a task is semantically entirely different from an server error. A server error indicates that the server encountered an unexpected issue or problem, whereas a task reject explicitly indicates that the task requested is out of scope for the agent. This is different from a typically HTTP error code, where maybe a parameter is misconfigured; this is the server agent _choosing_ not to do a task given its own internal policy for determining what work should be done.

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
- **Matt Pocock's Diagramming Intent:** [Matt Pocock](https://x.com/mattpocockuk), a well-known developer educator, [shared on X](https://x.com/mattpocockuk/status/1910002033018421400) "**I've just been reading the Agent2Agent technical docs - Google's new protocol for agent to agent communication. You know what that means. Let's diagram them:**". This tweet, liked and reposted hundreds of times, includes some great diagrams explaining the A2A protocol.
```

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
- **Craig McLuckie's "Hot Take":** [Craig McLuckie](https://www.linkedin.com/in/craigmcluckie/) shared his initial thoughts on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/craigmcluckie_hot-take-on-agent2agent-vs-mcp-google-just-activity-7315939233792176128-4rGQ) "**Hot take on Agent2Agent vs MCP**". His post highlighted Google's careful positioning of A2A as focused on interactions _between_ agentic systems, rather than agents interacting with resources (the focus of MCP). This distinction is crucial for improving models' ability to understand expectations from other agents. McLuckie also pointed out the potential for A2A to enable systems to **advertise specific capabilities and specialities**, which is seen as "**sensible**".
```

author: bobtista
raw_text:
Yeah, if I understand correctly, the code here is trying to use each tool like it's a micro service with its own URL, instead of using the MCP server. The agents using A2A can give each other tasks in plain english. Agents can use tools provided by connected MCP servers. 
Think of it as something like this:
Agent A asks Agent B "Hey what are your skills?" via A2A
"I'm good at getting the weather" 
Agent A asks Agent B "Ok what's the weather today in Paris?"
Agent B is connected to a weather MCP server.
Agent B submits a request to the weather MCP server to use its "get_weather" tool. (It got the list of available tools using the "tools/list" method)
Agent B gets the response from the weather MCP server and uses it to return the result to agent A as an artifact, completing the initial task.

author: bobtista
raw_text:
It's progress :) You don't need to create your own json rpc requests manually if you're using the imported MCP client - they are handling the json rpc for you. Also, you'd want to choose STDIO or SSE as the transport type for a given MCP server, and you don't need to make a new connection and list all tools on a per tool call basis. Finally, I'm still seeing code where you're trying to explicitly call a tool by its URL, and there still seems to be confusion about an MCP tool vs an MCP server. I'd recommend reading through he MCP docs, reviewing code from some sample MCP servers and clients, and using them locally eg in Claude Desktop to get a solid understanding. 

You may find that the A2A-MCP Connector concept isn't really a thing. An agent can use tools with MCP. It can also interact with other agents with A2A. They're both just kind of add ons to an agent. It seems to me that what you're making is an agent that is both A2A compliant and has an MCP client ready to use. You can make an example agent that is A2A compatible, and uses an MCP server eg the [git mcp server](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers/tree/main/src/git). This agent would be able to receive task requests from an A2A client in plain english like "Hey can you look at whatever files have changed and commit them with a brief commit message?" Or something more long lived, like if your commit automatically runs tests that take an hour, the A2A agent could let you know if the commit was rejected or not. The agent would use the tools that the git mcp server provides under the hood to accomplish these tasks. Does this all make sense?

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
To help with the review, let me explain why "default" values for error codes should not be in the spec.

The client should be able to validate received ErrorResponses using the A2A specification. However, the inclusion of the "default" codes leads to ErrorResponses with the missing `error.code` property to pass the validation (while such messages are invalid). So, this PR removes all "default" values from `error.code` definitions.

I think it is not as critical with the `eror.message` field, so I left them unchanged (to reduce the diff size).

author: darkhaniop
raw_text: Every JSON RPC response object must include the `id` property, which is used to correlate the response object with the corresponding request.

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
The situation with default values of the `method` property is similar to `error.code` (see above). The A2A server that receives RPC requests, first, should be able to validate the requests; second, it should be able to differentiate the request method based on the `method` property (instead of substituting a default value if it is missing).

Therefore, the spec should not state "default" values for the `method` property.

author: siacomuzzi
raw_text:
Currently, for demo purposes only, the wrapped tool waits until the user approves or rejects the push notification.
However, our plan for a future version of the sample is as follows (feedback is welcome):

1. After sending the push notification to the user, the wrapped tool stops execution by raising an interrupt (`GraphInterrupt` in the case of a LangGraph agent).

2. The "agent executor" returns the `auth-required` state to the A2A Client.

3. In parallel, have a separate worker monitor interrupted threads in LangGraph and attempt to resume them.

4. When the thread is resumed by the worker, whether because the user approved or rejected the notification, or because it expired and the tool completed the employment verification, we have two options:  
    a. Use the A2A push notification capability to inform the A2A Client of the employment verification result.
    b. Find a way (if supported by LangGraph) to prevent `graph.stream(...)` from stopping during step 1, so it can continue and emit the appropriate `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent` and `TaskStatusUpdateEvent`.

author: siacomuzzi
raw_text: Yes, when the user declines the CIBA auth request, the tool raises  a specific error and the "agent executor" sets the task status to "rejected".

stream message => role: user, type: text, text: Does John Doe with email jdoe@staff0.com work at Staff0?
stream message => role: agent, type: text, text: Looking up the employment status...
stream message => role: agent, type: text, text: Processing the employment status...
stream message => role: agent, type: text, text: Looking up the employment status...
stream message => role: agent, type: text, text: Processing the employment status...
stream message => role: agent, type: text, text: The user denied the authorization request
```

The SDK is designed to abstract away events being piped to user through stream events or push notif. The agent wouldn't need to care about it.

As mentioned by you, the difficult part would be to async handle the auth flows and emit events. One flow could be:
1. Wrap the graph.stream(), check if there's auth interrupt.
2. Manually Emit auth-required status event.
3. Wait on background worker to keep checking for CIBA auth. Maybe wait on an AsyncIO event queue. The queue would be populated by the worker.
4. I guess once we get the auth, resume the langgraph execution and stream the events.

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
From my understanding, JSON-RPC spec discourages the usage of id=null for successful responses. Also, in `types.ts`, this particular interface represents the "successful" response, because it defines `error?: never;` and is used only as a parent for `*SuccessResponse` interfaces.

With that in mind, what do you think about renaming `JSONRPCResult` to `JSONRPCSuccessResponse` (to mirror the `JSONRPCErrorResponse` interface)?

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text: I think these are mostly incorrect lint errors. Perhaps we should move the proto file to a v1 directory as per the first message, but otherwise I don't think these are an issue. We could have a separate `SendStreamingMessageRequest` type as input to the streaming message, I don't think we should as these two should have the exact same input contract and splitting will allow them to deviate. Similarly for the `StreamResponse` instead of two message for the response on the streaming call and the subscribe call. SetPushNotificationConfig: If not id is specified, then handler creates a new config and attaches to the task.
SetPushNotificationConfig: If id is specified, then this is treated as full update. Both url and auth will be validated and updated. Partial updates can be supported by fieldMask.

GetPushNotificationConfig: Gets all configs for a task.

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
Extensions are a means of extending the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol with new data, requirements, methods, and state machines. Agents declare their support for extensions in their [`AgentCard`](/specification/#5-agent-discovery-the-agent-card), and clients can then opt-in to the behavior offered by the extension as part of requests they make to the agent. Extensions are identified by a URI and defined by their extension specification. Anyone is able to define, publish, and implement an extension.
```

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
- Exposing new information in the `AgentCard`. An extension may not impact the request/response flow at all -- it can be simply used as a way to convey additional structured information to clients via the `AgentCard`. We refer to these as *data-only extensions*.
```

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
- Overlaying additional structure and state change requirements on the core request/response messages. An extension could, for example, require that all messages use `DataPart`s that adhere to a specific schema. This type of extension effectively acts as a profile on the core A2A protocol, narrowing the space of allowed values. We refer to these as *profile extensions*.
```

```

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
Try embedding the section of `types.ts` that has this information, or create a table similar to `specification.md`

[specification/#5-agent-discovery-the-agent-card](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#5521-agentextension-object)

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
When an `AgentCard` declares a required extension, this is a signal to clients that some aspect of the extension impacts how requests are structured. Agents should not mark data-only extensions as required, since there is no direct impact on how requests are made to the agent.
```

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
```suggestion
If an `AgentCard` declares a required extension, and the client does not request activation of that required extension, Agents should return reject the incoming request and return an appropriate error code.
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

While this change is functionally correct, the placement of the new `protocol_version` field goes against the Google Protocol Buffers Style Guide's recommendation for field ordering.

The style guide suggests ordering fields by their tag number to improve readability and maintainability. The new `protocol_version` field has tag `16`, which is the highest in the `AgentCard` message, so it should be placed at the end of the message definition.

I understand that other fields in this message are also out of order, but placing new fields correctly would be a good step towards improving the file's structure and maintainability.

**Suggestion:**

Please move this new field to the end of the `AgentCard` message definition, after the `supports_authenticated_extended_card` field.

Reference: [Google Protobuf Style Guide - Ordering](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/style#ordering)

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

There's an inconsistency in the pagination strategy between the JSON-RPC and gRPC specifications for `tasks/list`:

*   **JSON-RPC (`specification.md`, `a2a.json`)**: Uses `limit` and `offset`.
*   **gRPC (`a2a.proto`)**: Uses `page_size` and `page_token` (cursor-based pagination).

While `limit`/`offset` is common, it can have performance issues with large datasets (deep pagination), as skipping a large number of records can be inefficient for the database. Cursor-based pagination is generally more scalable.

Was this difference intentional? If so, it might be worth a note in the spec to explain the rationale. If not, for consistency and better performance characteristics, you might consider adopting a cursor-based approach for the JSON-RPC spec as well. This would involve replacing `offset` with something like `pageToken` (an opaque string value returned by the server) in both `ListTasksParams` and `ListTasksResult`.

Using the `union` merge strategy for lock files like `package-lock.json` and `yarn.lock` is not recommended. This strategy can silently create a corrupted lockfile by simply concatenating changes from different branches, which can lead to non-reproducible builds and dependency issues that are hard to track down.

It's generally safer to let Git handle merges for these files, even if it results in conflicts. A merge conflict forces a developer to resolve it properly, typically by regenerating the lockfile with `npm install` or `yarn install` after merging changes in `package.json`.

I suggest removing these two lines to rely on the default, safer merge behavior.

author: gemini-code-assist[bot]
raw_text:
![critical](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/critical.svg)

The logic inside `normalizeError` to map common error types is flawed. The local `code` variable is initialized to `A2AErrorCode.UNKNOWN_ERROR` and is then used in the `if` conditions' `.includes(code)` checks. Since `code` is `9999`, these checks will always be `false`, and the error code will never be re-mapped from `UNKNOWN_ERROR`.

The logic should inspect properties of the original `error` object (e.g., `error.code` or `error.message`) to determine the appropriate `A2AErrorCode`.

author: Fannon
raw_text:
```suggestion
          "type": "application/json",
```

This is maybe a general shortcoming of the API Catalog RFC, but the media type "application/json" in itself is not very descriptive. From this linkset result, you would not know that this describes an A2A Agent at all and also not that it's described via the Agent Card metadata format. To learn this, you have first to download the file and the file itself needs to be self-descriptive enough (have a discriminator what spec it is and what it describes).

But it's a very simple approach. 
For a more complex approach, based on experiences building cross-content, cross-technology catalogs, there is also the open-source [Open Resource Discovery](https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/specification/) specification, which does the same, but is more extensive and not just limited to APIs.

In case you have questions on that, feel free to get in touch.

author: gemini-code-assist[bot]
raw_text:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

There's a potential for a temporary file leak if an exception occurs between the creation of the temporary file and its movement to the destination. Wrap the file operations in a `try...finally` block to ensure the temporary file is always cleaned up, even if an error occurs. Additionally, consider normalizing the lines by stripping whitespace to avoid duplicates that only differ in whitespace.

```python
import os
import shutil
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path

def sort_unique_lines_in_file(file_path_str: str) -> None:
    file_path = Path(file_path_str)
    temp_file_obj = None
    try:
        with file_path.open('r', encoding='utf-8') as original_file:
            unique_lines = sorted({line.strip() for line in original_file if line.strip()})

        temp_file_obj = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
            mode='w', encoding='utf-8', delete=False, dir=file_path.parent
        )
        with open(temp_file_obj.name, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as temp_file:
            temp_file.writelines(unique_lines)

        shutil.move(temp_file_obj.name, file_path)
        print(f'Successfully sorted and updated {file_path.name}')
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print(f'Error: The file {file_path} was not found.')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'An unexpected error occurred: {e}')
    finally:
        if temp_file_obj:
            try:
                os.remove(temp_file_obj.name)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Failed to remove temporary file: {e}")
```

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Thank you for the thoughtful comment — I completely agree that simply using `application/json` as a media type is not sufficiently descriptive.

As a pragmatic interim step, using a more specific media type such as `application/vnd.a2a.agent-card+json` can serve as a practical workaround.

Incidentally, there are already active discussions on related topics — such as the IANA registration of `.well-known` URIs — for example, in [protocol-registries/well-known-uris#66](https://github.com/protocol-registries/well-known-uris/issues/66). I expect similar conversations around media types and schema identification will emerge as the specifications mature.

Regarding ORD — I’ve reviewed the documentation and find it to be a very well-designed and extensible protocol. I believe it has strong potential to inform and support open discovery initiatives.

That said, I’d like to raise two considerations:

 - **SAP ecosystem focus:** While ORD is open, it appears to have been primarily designed with usage in the SAP ecosystem in mind. In contrast, A2A’s design goal is to remain vendor-neutral. Standards implicitly tied to a specific platform may hinder broader adoption.
 - **Complexity vs. minimalism:** ORD offers rich and expressive metadata, which is impressive. However, our current goal here with A2A Open Discovery is to propose a minimal, widely interoperable mechanism. From this perspective, ORD might currently be more comprehensive than necessary.

That said, we believe ORD’s architectural insights could be highly valuable — not only for Open Discovery but also for ongoing efforts such as the Agent Catalog and Registry:

https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/discussions/741

If you’re open to it, we’d love to have you join the conversation!

author: Fannon
raw_text:
@ognis1205 Thanks for replying so quickly and your feedback!
Yes, defining a more specific media type for agent-cards would address the concerns, then it's at least clear what links are pointing to agent cards. Maybe `application/agent-card+json` could also be sufficient? Then it's closer to your proposed `.well-known/agent-card.json`. 

I fully agree with your complexity vs. minimalism argument here, that's why I think you're making the right choice when going with this simpler approach. Something like ORD becomes only attractive when you're building a catalog / metadata architecture that combines many types of protocols and metadata and you want to connect them and govern them together. 
Yes, ORD is currently focused on the SAP ecosystem, but we recently donated it to Linux Foundation and I hope it will grow beyond it. Being vendor-neutral is a design-goal of ORD as well, but clearly it grew out of SAPs specific requirements. 

I'd be happy to join the conversation on it, exchange experiences and ideas. Thanks!

author: Fannon
raw_text:
@ognis1205  - since you plan to change the .well-known URI of agent cards anyway, have you thought about allowing multiple agent cards being returned in one JSON file there? 

It may also not possible for everyone to implement a `.well-known/...` URI on their root, because they may not have access to that. In that case, there has to be some kind of fallback - like registering your full config URL at the registry to collect it, or some means to push the agent cards to a registry.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Thanks for your thoughts, @Fannon !

As a matter of fact, the proposal and discussion in [#166](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/discussions/166) actually originated from a very similar idea — exposing a list of agent cards under the `.well-known` path.

While returning the agent cards directly from `.well-known` is a simple and fast approach, I’ve come to believe there are some advantages in using a more scalable design. That’s why the current proposal suggests returning only the list of endpoints (where each individual Agent Card can be retrieved) — specifically in the form of an [API Catalog](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9727/)-like format.

This has two main benefits:

 - It allows for better scalability and maintenance, since each Agent Card can be managed independently.
 - It enables domain non-owners (e.g., project contributors) to keep their cards up-to-date, as long as the domain owner lists their endpoints once.

As for uploading or notifying the registry of an Agent Card — if done via Open Discovery — using this API Catalog format should suffice. For other methods (such as when `.well-known` is not available), that probably falls into the scope of Agent Registry or Agent Catalog API/protocols, which we may want to define separately.

author: Fannon
raw_text:
I see. With this approach, you also return the agent cards under a `.well-known` path, just with the difference that you first return a list of links, which point to the agent cards (one indirection). 

But I agree that this approach scales better for bigger scenarios, because you first have a smaller list of everything that is available and then you can retrieve the metadata for just those resources you are really interested in. That's basically how it works in ORD, too. 

Question: This format is meant only for publishing agent card to the registry - or would the registry itself also have the same implementation to discover what's inside of it? Or will this be a separate, dedicated API?

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Thanks for the thoughtful question.

A few clarifications:
 1. With this format, Agent Cards are not required to live under the `.well-known` path. The `href` fields in the response can point to any arbitrary path, which gives publishers flexibility in structuring their resources.
 2. The API Catalog format is based on IETF specifications for Open API discovery. It’s not designed solely for publishing Agent Cards to a registry. Any third party aware of this format can consume and use the information for their own purposes. Whether a registry adopts the same discovery mechanism internally is entirely up to the registry implementer.
  3. I understand the name "API Catalog" might be a bit misleading — it’s not an actual API or a dedicated endpoint for serving data like a typical REST-style service. Instead, it refers to a declarative document that catalogs available agent interfaces in a structured format. So it’s conceptually very different from something like a REST catalog or a discoverable API registry.

If the goal is to discuss this in the context of the ORD project and registry discovery, I’d strongly recommend continuing the conversation in the Agent Registry discussion I shared earlier. This PR, from a discovery standpoint, is orthogonal to the registry: it focuses on **open** discovery specifically.

The current A2A Protocol spec assumes a "one agent per host" model for open discovery. This proposal tries to relax that constraint by introducing a more flexible alternative. So rather than being about publishing to the registry or registry introspection, it’s about enabling more expressive `.well-known` discovery for individual agents or agent collections hosted under the same domain.

That said, I feel there might be a slight misalignment between what we’re proposing in this PR and the assumptions behind your question. We’re happy to clarify further if needed!

author: ognis1205
ognis1205
raw_text:
I’m still learning about ORD and may be missing some important context, but I find it to be a well-thought-out specification.
My goal is to suggest something simple and fair for the broader community, and I’d love to continue learning and discussing this with you—would that be okay?

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Regarding the questions you raised:

> This format is meant only for publishing agent card to the registry?

At the time of drafting this PR, discussions around an Agent Registry or Catalog had not yet begun. The primary motivation was to address a limitation in the A2A Protocol — specifically, its implicit assumption of a one-agent-per-host model for open discovery.

Therefore, this format is not designed solely for publishing Agent Cards to a registry. Rather, it is intended as a general-purpose mechanism to support open discovery, including but not limited to registry scenarios.

> Or would the registry itself also have the same implementation to discover what’s inside of it?

This PR does not assume that registries will adopt the exact same implementation or format for introspection. In fact, my original thinking was that a registry would likely expose a different protocol, with more emphasis on authentication, validation, and governance, which are outside the scope of open discovery.

That said, registries could choose to consume these open discovery documents — but doing so is entirely up to their implementation.

> Or will this be a separate, dedicated API?

Yes — that was the original intention when drafting this proposal. I envisioned registry interactions as being handled via a separate and dedicated API, distinct from the open discovery format described here.

author: Fannon
raw_text:
> @Fannon
> 
> https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/specification/spec-v1/interfaces/document-api
> 
> Is this where I can find the official specification for the Open Discovery document API in ORD?

Yes, but the bulk is actually in the ORD Document interface: https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/specification/spec-v1/interfaces/document 

Most of it is not relevant here, but it has a collection of "API Resources", where you can describe the existence of APIs of various protocol types incl. MCP or A2A (soon). But the actual metadata definitions are provided as a link, e.g. to an Agent Card file, or an OpenAPI document - whatever is relevant to the protocols. 
Technically it's 3 steps: First the `.well-known/open-resource-discovery`, which lists the ORD documents (and how they can be accessed), second the ORD documents and third the various metadata definition files (usually existing industry standards)

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
@Fannon , sorry for the late reply.

Yes, personally I take a relatively open stance regarding the format used under `.well-known` for Open Discovery. That said, the reason I advocate for the API Catalog format is because:

 - It is extremely simple and only provides the minimal necessary functionality.
 - The schema definition is based on a web standard (linkset), allowing us to define the specification without reinventing or extending existing ones.
 - The RFC explicitly discusses how to operate when coexisting with other formats, particularly through content negotiation.

Regarding the last point, although I don’t fully understand all the details of the ORD specification yet, I believe that—even if the format doesn’t use the recommended path `.well-known/open-resource-discovery`—as long as it uses a proper Content-Type (e.g., `application/vnd.sap.ord+json`) and that type is accepted by the community, it should be possible to support ORD-compliant Open Agent Discovery via API Catalog specification.

That said, I’m open to continuing this discussion further—and perhaps this is not just about Open Discovery, but also a good opportunity to discuss the broader protocol around Agent Catalogs and Registries as well.

SEE ALSO:
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9727/

author: Fannon
raw_text:
@ognis1205 - right now we don't have a defined media type for ORD documents, but maybe it's a good idea to define this for the reason you outlined!

Allthough we then have the situation that there are many `.well-known` entry points. If you support a specific discovery mechanism, you would anyway go for the corresponding well-known URI, e.g. if you want to discover A2A agent cards, you might want to try with `.well-known/agent-card.json` first anyway - but then there's the limitation that you can get only one. So you try with `/.well-known/api-catalog.json`, where you can get more than one A2A agent card, but also potentially links to other API definitions like OpenAPI. 

For ORD, you start with `/.well-known/open-resource-discovery`, which would work the same as the api-catalog, but have more information about the APIs on ORD level already and support describing other aspects about the application. So I'm not sure if we would need to interlink the discovery mechanisms, as each has a well-known entry-point anyway. But I would expect that you would share the same link to the same definition files, e.g. the agent-card JSON files.

Or do you see value of linking those discovery mechanisms more closely together? E.g. from ORD config side, we could point to a `/.well-known/api-catalog.json` - but not sure of the value in it, as it would not contain more information than we would have in ORD already. And if you don't understand ORD, you wouldn't have called its entry point in the first place. (Just thinking by writing)

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
@Fannon , thanks for the thoughtful response — I realize that my PR, or the documentation so far, might not have clearly communicated some of the assumptions or motivations behind the proposal. If you don’t mind, I’d like to clarify three specific points that may have caused misunderstanding:

1. .well-known/api-catalog (not .json)

It might seem like a minor detail, but the proposed API Catalog endpoint is actually `.well-known/api-catalog`, not `.well-known/api-catalog.json`.

This is important in the context of content negotiation, as described in [RFC 9727 Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9727#section-4.2):

> The Publisher MAY make additional formats available via content negotiation to their /.well-known/api-catalog location.

So while `application/linkset+json` is the default required format, servers can serve alternative representations via standard content negotiation. This is a central design point of this proposal.

2. Multiple Open Discovery Endpoints: Rationale

Part of the motivation behind this proposal stems from concerns around the current `.well-known/agent.json` endpoint, which implicitly assumes a one-agent-per-host model. I believe this is problematic, and would recommend either:

 - removing the .well-known-based discovery from the A2A spec entirely, or
 - replacing it with a more flexible mechanism (such as this PR proposes).

Note that I intentionally left `.well-known/agent.json` out of the scope of this PR to keep the discussion focused.
That said, it’s my opinion that the need for `.well-known/agent.json` could become obsolete if the community decides to adopt `.well-known/api-catalog` — though this is certainly open to discussion.

So, while you mentioned the possibility of clients trying both `.well-known/agent-card.json` and `.well-known/api-catalog` — if we go with the catalog-based discovery, I would argue that `.well-known/agent.json` should not be recommended or implemented at all.

The choice of discovery mechanism should be left up to the Agent Provider, and the spec should RECOMMEND—but not REQUIRE—any specific one.

3. Not Interlinking Discovery Mechanisms — but Supporting Content Negotiation

Finally, to clarify one more possible misunderstanding:

I’m not proposing interlinking different discovery mechanisms (e.g., linking from ORD to API Catalog or vice versa). Instead, I’m suggesting content negotiation as the unifying mechanism.

To illustrate, a client could perform:

```
GET https://agent.org/.well-known/api-catalog  
Accept: application/vnd.sap.ord+json
```

In this way, a single `.well-known/api-catalog` endpoint can support ORD or any other discovery format — without needing separate entry points.

This does not contradict the ORD spec, and in fact, enables ORD-based discovery to be bootstrapped from a single catalog endpoint — while remaining compatible with RFC 9727.

cc @darrelmiller — If you happen to have time, I’d be grateful for your perspective, especially if I’ve misunderstood any part of API Catalog or its use of content negotiation.


> Although we then have the situation that there are many .well-known entry points…

You’re right — proliferation of .well-known endpoints can be a concern.
However, just to clarify: the proposed path is `.well-known/api-catalog` (not .json), and supports [content negotiation per RFC 9727 §4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9727#section-4.2). This allows a single endpoint to serve multiple representations — so clients don’t need to try multiple `.well-known/*` paths.

> For ORD, you start with /.well-known/open-resource-discovery, which would work the same as the api-catalog…

Actually, you don’t need to use `.well-known/open-resource-discovery` if `.well-known/api-catalog` supports content negotiation. For example, a client could send:

```
GET /.well-known/api-catalog  
Accept: application/vnd.sap.ord+json
```

This enables API Catalog to serve as the single discovery entry point — even for ORD — without violating the ORD spec.

> So I’m not sure if we would need to interlink the discovery mechanisms…

Agreed — and to clarify, this proposal does not suggest interlinking between mechanisms.
Instead, it promotes content negotiation from a single `.well-known/api-catalog` as a clean, extensible solution. Each protocol can co-exist via negotiated media types, without needing explicit links between them.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
As an additional note, while the specific schema for this has not yet been defined, I personally believe that even when relying on an external Agent Registry, it should be possible to expose the Registry’s endpoint as part of Open Discovery by leveraging the linkset format used in the API Catalog.

In other words, linkset allows us to declaratively indicate where clients can interact with a registry—making it a natural part of the discovery mechanism itself. This could help unify discovery and registration workflows, even across multiple registry implementations.

If this approach proves viable, `.well-known/api-catalog` could serve not only as the central entry point for Open Discovery, but also as a bootstrap layer for federated Agent Registry infrastructure in the future.

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
I don't think we should define JSONRPC as a required transport for an A2A server. There can be scenarios where certain customers would only want to use gRPC or some custom transport (e.g. PubSub) and don't even need JSON-RPC and a capable client can still communicate with the server and exchange A2A types.

A server can choose to support one or more transport types and any clients that support any of the transports can communicate with the server. The client sdks we provide should be capable of communicating via default supported transport types (JSONRPC, gRPC, REST) and if a customer chooses to build a different transport, they can do so by implementing the custom transport for the client and server interfaces.

author: maeste
raw_text:
I see, it was an assumption of minimal transport to support defining a server compliant. I'll review the PR the other way, not having any of them required and defining compliance per transport. So, the TCK will test on one or more transports (of the ones defined in the spec) and guarantee compliance for each transport.
I agree some customers want to rely on specific transport (e.g gRPC) or custom transport, but OTH, if we want A2A as an interoperability protocol, it is also important to define compliance on the standard transports mentioned in the spec.

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Sounds good on TCK.

Transport types defined in the spec should work for most, but customers should be able to define custom transport types specific to their needs and still be interoperable exchanging A2A types as long a client can support the transport.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
@Fannon 

Additional note:

> So I’m not sure if we would need to interlink the discovery mechanisms…

I also don’t believe that simply interlinking various Open Discovery Mechanisms inherently provides much value. At this stage, I think it’s perfectly reasonable for each provider—or the A2A community at large—to adopt whichever Open Discovery Mechanism best suits their network scale or preferences.

That said, once the surrounding technologies in the A2A ecosystem mature further, I do think there remains room for discussion—and perhaps open questions—on how the A2A protocol, or a future A2A Discovery protocol, might formally incorporate or standardize the use of Open Discovery mechanisms.

```

author: Fannon
raw_text:
@ognis1205 sorry for the late reply. 

I'm not sure it makes sense to include ORD into the .well-known API catalog discovery, because ORD has a wider scope, it's not just an API catalog, it describes an application / service to external consumers in general. And since ORD already has a .well-known URI, there wouldn't be a real advantage of putting it in there, imho. 

But that doesn't really affect your PR / decisions here, since A2A agent cards do fall well within the api-catalog scope.

> That said, it’s my opinion that the need for .well-known/agent.json could become obsolete if the community decides to adopt .well-known/api-catalog — though this is certainly open to discussion.

Yes, I think this decision need to be made. The `.well-known/api-catalog` is more versatile and can also mesh with other API discovery. I would agree that it makes the `.well-known/agent.json` / `.well-known/agent-card.json` obsolete. To avoid confusions, it may be a good idea to deprecate it in favor of the `.well-known/api-catalog`.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Thanks for the thoughtful input, @Fannon ! Don’t worry about the late reply.

Just to clarify, I’m not at all opposed to ORD. That said, my priorities around changes in this area are roughly the following:

 1. Update the language in the [current spec](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/agent-discovery/#discovery-strategies)—specifically, this part:
 
> This is a recommended approach for public agents or agents intended for broad discoverability within a specific domain.

I’d suggest adjusting the wording to reflect that there are now multiple valid `.well-known`-based approaches, including ORD and API Catalog. Among those, the A2A community has chosen to register `.well-known/agent-card.json` with IANA, and links to other recommended options could also be provided for broader context.

 2. Encourage use of a `.well-known`-based discovery mechanism that supports multi-agent scenarios under a single host, such as ORD or the API Catalog. The spec could recommend one of these options and provide a formal schema, as this PR begins to do.

Of these, I personally think (1) would be the most pragmatic and forward-compatible approach for the A2A community.

I’ll try to open a discussion thread later on to explore this direction more collaboratively.
